title: The bar exam and the legends of the bar

audience_match: Law students, bar reviewees, and 2024 bar takers
Law-school deans, faculty, and PALS network
Members of the Philippine bench and bar
Bar-exam committee members and SC justices
Diokno, Salonga, and Teehankee family circles
FEU, UP, and Ateneo alumni networks

primary_topics: Bar-exam statistics across decades (1960 CJ's batch; 1965-1981 small numbers; 1999 lowest 16.59%; 2021 digitized record 8,241 passers; 72.28% pass rate)

CJ's dissolution of his own law firm (Panganiban, Parlade, Benitez, and Africa) on SC appointment in 1995, emulating Salonga's 1986 PCGG dissolution

signature_phrases.phrase: The three-day 2024 Bar exam was finished yesterday
When I took the Bar exam in 1960, we were only 4,178, and 1,667 passed
It was held on four consecutive Sundays (instead of three days in just one week) and in only one location, the University of the East
THE LOWEST PASSING RATE was recorded in 1999 at 16.59 percent
An attempt to increase the percentage via the lowering of the passing grade from 75 to 74 percent was scuttled because of my strong objections
rules should be obeyed and should not be changed midway
the bar exam chair (who was an associate justice) was reprimanded and his honorarium as chair slashed by half
for failing to disclose that a nephew of his took the Bar exam
The most numerous examinees were in 2021 when 11,402 took the digitized and regionalized tests for the first time
8,241 passed, the most in history
With due respect, I disagree with this wholesale entry to the doors of the legal profession
must swing on reluctant hinges
AFTER ALL, OUR PROFESSION IS THE FIRST KEY TO THE JUSTICE SYSTEM
Lawyering is invested in the public interest
lawyers are regarded and disciplined by the Supreme Court as public officers
Just because a few lawyers lie, people generalize the label to all
I also disagree with the nondisclosure of the top 10 passers
Notably, of the 27 chief justices of the Philippines
excluding Maria Lourdes Sereno who was ousted via quo warranto and deemed never to have been a de jure chief justice
at least 11 are bar placers
SINCE MY STUDENT DAYS, I have idealized three bar topnotchers
Jose W. Diokno, Jovito R. Salonga, and Claudio Teehankee
Diokno and Salonga tied for first place in the 1944 Bar exam with identical grades of 95.3 percent
Amazingly, Diokno did not finish law school. (He also topped the CPA board exam.)
Salonga was my mentor at Far Eastern University, my boss at his law firm, and my lifetime guru
He was in awe of only two lawyers, Diokno and Teehankee
Please prepare our trial brief extra well because Pepe Diokno (or Dingdong Teehankee) is our opponent
Teehankee had impeccable academic credentials: summa cum laude in both his Bachelor of Arts and Bachelor of Laws from Ateneo de Manila, and numero uno in the 1940 Bar exam
he remained independent and wrote (or concurred with) decisions unfavorable to him
In my humble view, he is the greatest Ateneo law alumnus of all time
I tried, though unworthy, to emulate these three great masters
I dissolved my own law firm (Panganiban, Parlade, Benitez, and Africa) upon my appointment to the Supreme Court in 1995
The dissolution orphaned my law partners
Please excuse my feeble attempts to imitate these legends of the bar as I humbly try, though undeserving, to live in the penumbra of their great shadows

institutions: University of the East (UE) â€” 1960 bar venue
Presidential Commission on Good Government (PCGG) â€” Salonga 1986
Panganiban, Parlade, Benitez, and Africa Law Office
events: 1944 Bar exam â€” Diokno and Salonga tie at 95.3%
1986 â€” Salonga's dissolution of his law firm on PCGG appointment
1995 â€” CJ's dissolution of his law firm on SC appointment
1999 â€” lowest pass rate; SC reprimand of bar-exam chair
2021 â€” first digitized regionalized bar; record passers

stances.claim: The 2024 three-day bar exam closed Sept 15 with 12,246 applicants and 10,483 first-day appearances â€” over five times the 1960 batch (4,178; 1,667 passed; four consecutive Sundays at UE) and the small 1965-1981 cohorts of under 2,000.

The 1999 record-low 16.59% pass rate (660 passers) prompted an attempt to lower the passing grade from 75 to 74 percent, which CJ â€” then an incumbent associate justice â€” strongly objected to ('rules should be obeyed and should not be changed midway'); the bar-exam chair was also reprimanded and his honorarium halved for failing to disclose that a nephew of his was taking the bar.
The 2021 digitized regionalized exam produced 8,241 passers â€” the most in history â€” with a 72.28% pass rate, also a historical high; for the first time, the top 10 passers were not named.

With due respect, CJ disagrees with this 'wholesale entry to the doors of the legal profession' â€” 'must swing on reluctant hinges' â€” even under the COVID-19 emergency; the SC should remain wary and admit only the most qualified, because the legal profession is the first key to the justice system and lawyers are 'invested in the public interest' and disciplined by the SC as public officers.

CJ also disagrees with the nondisclosure of the top 10 passers (a practice now abandoned in the last two years); topping the bar is no guarantee of legal excellence, but top 10 placers nonetheless become great practitioners, professors, corporate counsels, and government officials sought after by top firms.

Of the 27 Philippine CJs (excluding ousted-via-quo-warranto Sereno deemed never a de jure CJ), at least 11 are bar placers: Yulo (3rd), Paras (2nd), Bengzon (2nd), Concepcion (1st), Makalintal (7th), R. Aquino (9th), Teehankee (1st), Yap (1st), Narvasa (2nd), Panganiban (6th), and Bersamin (9th).

CJ has idealized three bar legends since student days â€” Diokno and Salonga tying for #1 in 1944 at 95.3% (Diokno did not finish law school; also topped CPA boards); Teehankee (Ateneo summa BA and LLB; 1940 #1; remained independent despite his Marcos appointment).

Salonga was CJ's FEU mentor, law-firm boss, and lifetime guru, and was in awe of only two lawyers â€” Diokno and Teehankee â€” instructing CJ when either was on the opposite side to prepare the trial brief extra well; in emulation of Salonga's 1986 PCGG-appointment law-firm dissolution, CJ dissolved his own firm (Panganiban, Parlade, Benitez, and Africa) on his 1995 SC appointment, orphaning his bar-placer partners.

notable_anecdotes.summary: The exact contrast â€” '1960, we were only 4,178, and 1,667 passedâ€¦ four consecutive Sundaysâ€¦ in only one location, the University of the Eastâ€¦ 1965-1981, less than 2,000 took the exam. Now, more than five times did.' â€” six decades collapsed into a single statistical paragraph.

The 1999 nepotism-and-passing-grade story â€” CJ's strong objection scuttling the 75-to-74 lowering, plus the SC's reprimand of the bar-exam chair (an AJ) whose nephew was a taker â€” a rare personal-institutional anecdote of the kind CJ reserves for principled-disagreement contexts.
The 'must swing on reluctant hinges' phrase for the legal profession â€” one of CJ's most memorable single-image formulations of bar-admission gatekeeping.
The exact roster of 11 chief-justice bar placers with placement numbers â€” CJ explicitly self-inscribing as 'Panganiban (sixth)' â€” turning a historical observation into a quiet biographical claim.
The Diokno-Salonga 1944 tie at 95.3% (Diokno did not finish law school; also topped CPA boards) â€” a single line that contains one of the most striking facts in Philippine legal history.

The Salonga in-awe-of-only-two-lawyers detail â€” 'Please prepare our trial brief extra well because Pepe Diokno (or Dingdong Teehankee) is our opponent' â€” preserving the affectionate first-name diminutives ('Pepe,' 'Dingdong') used between bar legends.
The Teehankee 'greatest Ateneo law alumnus of all time' claim â€” paired with his independence despite owing his appointment to Marcos â€” used as CJ's exemplar of integrity-after-appointment.

The 1995 law-firm dissolution detail â€” orphaning Dean Agustin Benitez (1959 #1), Dean Custodio Parlade (1959 #4), and classmate Leopoldo Africa (1960 #11) â€” each partner identified with his bar placement number, making the dissolution itself a memorial roster.
The closing 'feeble attemptsâ€¦ in the penumbra of their great shadows' â€” CJ's signature humility-disclaimer applied to his own bar-legends emulation.

decision_framework_signals: applies the 'rules must be obeyed and not changed midway' principle to the 1999 passing-grade controversy
uses the 'reluctant hinges' gatekeeping principle for bar admission
applies the 'lawyering is invested in the public interest' framing as the warrant for stringent admission standards
uses the disclosure-of-top-10 transparency principle (against record-suppression)
applies the integrity-after-appointment principle (Teehankee remaining independent despite owing his appointment to Marcos)
uses the law-firm-dissolution-on-appointment convention (Salonga 1986 PCGG; CJ 1995 SC) as the model of independence-protection
applies the imitation-of-models close as the operative personal-emulation framework
uses the 'penumbra of their great shadows' humility-aware self-positioning

one_paragraph_summary: On the closure of the three-day 2024 Bar exam (Sept 8, 11, 15), Panganiban reflects on a profession that must 'swing on reluctant hinges.' His own 1960 batch was 4,178 with 1,667 passers, held over four consecutive Sundays at the University of the East; yearly 1965-1981 cohorts numbered under 2,000; the lowest pass rate was 16.59% in 1999, when an attempt to lower the passing grade from 75 to 74 percent was scuttled by CJ's strong objections â€” and the bar-exam chair was reprimanded and his honorarium halved for failing to disclose that a nephew of his was a taker. The most numerous batch was 2021's 11,402 in the first digitized regionalized exam, with a record 8,241 passers at 72.28% and (for the first time) the top 10 unnamed. With due respect, CJ disagrees with this wholesale entry â€” the legal profession is the first key to the justice system and lawyers are public officers â€” and with the nondisclosure of top 10 placers (since abandoned), because while bar topping is no guarantee of excellence, top 10 placers nonetheless become leading practitioners, professors, corporate counsels, and government officials. Of the 27 Philippine CJs (excluding ousted-via-quo-warranto Sereno, deemed never a de jure CJ), at least 11 are bar placers: Yulo (3rd), Paras (2nd), Bengzon (2nd), Concepcion (1st), Makalintal (7th), R. Aquino (9th), Teehankee (1st), Yap (1st), Narvasa (2nd), Panganiban (6th), and Bersamin (9th). Since student days, CJ has idealized three bar legends: Jose W. Diokno and Jovito R. Salonga, tied at 95.3% in 1944 â€” and amazingly Diokno did not finish law school (and also topped the CPA boards); Salonga was CJ's FEU mentor, law-office boss, and lifetime guru, in awe of only Diokno and Teehankee ('Please prepare our trial brief extra well because Pepe Diokno or Dingdong Teehankee is our opponent'); and Claudio Teehankee, Ateneo summa BA and LLB and 1940 bar #1, who remained independent despite his Marcos appointment â€” 'in my humble view, the greatest Ateneo law alumnus of all time.' In emulation of Salonga's 1986 PCGG-appointment law-firm dissolution, CJ dissolved his own firm Panganiban, Parlade, Benitez, and Africa on his 1995 SC appointment, orphaning Dean Agustin Benitez (1959 #1), Dean Custodio Parlade (1959 #4), and classmate Leopoldo Africa (1960 #11). 'Please excuse my feeble attempts to imitate these legends of the bar as I humbly try, though undeserving, to live in the penumbra of their great shadows.'
